ENMThailand 0.1.0
  • Reference
    • Day 1 — Virtual species with nicheR
    • Day 2a — Downloading the workshop data
    • Day 2b — Environmental thinning with bean
    • Day 3 — Temporally-explicit SDMs with TemporalModelR

Day 1 — Virtual species with nicheR

Source: vignettes/Day1_nicheR.Rmd
Day1_nicheR.Rmd

Overview

Workshop navigation — part 1 of 4 in the ENM-Thailand 2026 workshop.

  • Day 1 (this page) — Virtual species with nicheR. Build an ellipsoid niche by hand and project it.
  • Day 2a — Downloading the workshop data. Fetch Thailand, WorldClim and GBIF Sambar records.
  • Day 2b — Environmental thinning with bean. Reduce E-space sampling bias before fitting a real niche.
  • Day 3 — Temporally-explicit SDMs with TemporalModelR. Pair each occurrence with the environment it experienced.

nicheR is an R package for building and visualising ellipsoid-based ecological niches in environmental space (E-space) and projecting them onto geographic space (G-space). Today we treat the niche as a hypothesis we control — a virtual species — so we never need any real occurrence data.

This is the ideal way to learn ENM mechanics, because we can compare every output back to a known truth.

Today we will:

  1. Build an ellipsoid from a user-defined range of bioclimatic tolerances.
  2. Explore the environmental space of Thailand in 3D using BIO1, BIO12, and BIO15.
  3. Project the ellipsoid onto Thailand bioclim rasters to get suitability and Mahalanobis distance.
  4. Add a sampling-bias layer and sample virtual occurrence points using three sampling strategies (centroid, random, edge).

Install and load packages

The chunk below installs everything Day 1 needs the first time you run it. Re-running is safe — already-installed packages are skipped.

# ---- CRAN dependencies -----------------------------------------------------
cran_pkgs <- c(
  "terra",
  "sf",
  "plotly",
  "viridis",
  "rmarkdown",
  "knitr",
  "remotes"
)
to_install <- cran_pkgs[!cran_pkgs %in% rownames(installed.packages())]
if (length(to_install)) install.packages(to_install, dependencies = TRUE)

# ---- nicheR from GitHub ----------------------------------------------------
if (!requireNamespace("nicheR", quietly = TRUE)) {
  remotes::install_github("castanedaM/nicheR", upgrade = "never")
}
library(nicheR)
library(terra)
library(sf)
library(plotly)

# Reproducible random draws throughout the lesson
set.seed(2026)

Data

For Day 1 we work with the WorldClim bioclim rasters and the Thailand polygon. These files are produced by Day 2a (Day2a_Data_Download.Rmd) — please knit Day 2a once before Day 1 so the files exist in data/processed/.

bio_path <- "data/processed/bioclim/bioclim_thailand.tif"
tha_path <- "data/processed/boundary/thailand_boundary.gpkg"

bioclim <- rast(bio_path)
thailand <- st_read(tha_path, quiet = TRUE)

plot(thailand[2])

# Show layer names — bio1 … bio19
names(bioclim)
##  [1] "bio1"  "bio2"  "bio3"  "bio4"  "bio5"  "bio6"  "bio7"  "bio8"  "bio9" 
## [10] "bio10" "bio11" "bio12" "bio13" "bio14" "bio15" "bio16" "bio17" "bio18"
## [19] "bio19"
summary(bioclim)
##       bio1            bio2             bio3            bio4       
##  Min.   :20.53   Min.   : 6.737   Min.   :45.89   Min.   : 45.57  
##  1st Qu.:25.23   1st Qu.: 9.157   1st Qu.:53.61   1st Qu.:127.17  
##  Median :26.55   Median :10.508   Median :56.56   Median :198.82  
##  Mean   :26.15   Mean   :10.129   Mean   :58.98   Mean   :184.96  
##  3rd Qu.:27.24   3rd Qu.:11.088   3rd Qu.:62.05   3rd Qu.:239.76  
##  Max.   :28.90   Max.   :12.370   Max.   :82.32   Max.   :338.24  
##  NA's   :2634    NA's   :2634     NA's   :2634    NA's   :2634    
##       bio5            bio6             bio7            bio8      
##  Min.   :29.04   Min.   : 8.599   Min.   : 9.30   Min.   :21.76  
##  1st Qu.:33.29   1st Qu.:14.324   1st Qu.:14.07   1st Qu.:25.83  
##  Median :34.55   Median :16.836   Median :18.94   Median :26.87  
##  Mean   :34.46   Mean   :16.821   Mean   :17.64   Mean   :26.59  
##  3rd Qu.:35.72   3rd Qu.:19.446   3rd Qu.:20.35   3rd Qu.:27.64  
##  Max.   :38.14   Max.   :24.400   Max.   :23.56   Max.   :28.82  
##  NA's   :2634    NA's   :2634     NA's   :2634    NA's   :2634   
##       bio9           bio10           bio11           bio12          bio13      
##  Min.   :16.69   Min.   :23.13   Min.   :16.69   Min.   : 806   Min.   :170.0  
##  1st Qu.:22.16   1st Qu.:27.44   1st Qu.:22.04   1st Qu.:1121   1st Qu.:218.0  
##  Median :24.37   Median :28.38   Median :23.94   Median :1289   Median :248.0  
##  Mean   :23.94   Mean   :28.16   Mean   :23.60   Mean   :1491   Mean   :289.1  
##  3rd Qu.:25.90   3rd Qu.:29.19   3rd Qu.:25.47   3rd Qu.:1754   3rd Qu.:323.2  
##  Max.   :29.09   Max.   :30.56   Max.   :28.24   Max.   :4101   Max.   :876.0  
##  NA's   :2634    NA's   :2634    NA's   :2634    NA's   :2634   NA's   :2634   
##      bio14            bio15            bio16            bio17       
##  Min.   : 0.000   Min.   : 37.47   Min.   : 426.0   Min.   :  8.00  
##  1st Qu.: 3.000   1st Qu.: 78.22   1st Qu.: 555.0   1st Qu.: 19.00  
##  Median : 4.000   Median : 83.06   Median : 647.0   Median : 23.00  
##  Mean   : 9.628   Mean   : 81.19   Mean   : 748.3   Mean   : 47.74  
##  3rd Qu.: 6.000   3rd Qu.: 86.59   3rd Qu.: 840.0   3rd Qu.: 36.00  
##  Max.   :90.000   Max.   :109.94   Max.   :2530.0   Max.   :372.00  
##  NA's   :2634     NA's   :2634     NA's   :2634     NA's   :2634    
##      bio18            bio19        
##  Min.   : 157.0   Min.   :  13.00  
##  1st Qu.: 281.0   1st Qu.:  26.00  
##  Median : 344.0   Median :  42.00  
##  Mean   : 375.0   Mean   : 137.50  
##  3rd Qu.: 408.2   3rd Qu.:  69.25  
##  Max.   :1896.0   Max.   :1164.00  
##  NA's   :2634     NA's   :2634

For a clean teaching example we work with three bioclimatic variables. These also drive the 3-D environmental exploration in the next step:

  • bio1 — annual mean temperature (°C, WorldClim v2.1)
  • bio12 — annual precipitation (mm)
  • bio15 — precipitation seasonality (CV)
env3    <- bioclim[[c("bio1", "bio12", "bio15")]]
env3_df <- as.data.frame(env3, na.rm = TRUE)
plot(env3)

Step 1 — Explore E-space in 3D (BIO1 × BIO12 × BIO15)

Before defining a niche, look at the available environmental conditions of Thailand in three dimensions. BIO15 (precipitation seasonality, CV) captures within-year rainfall variability and adds an important third axis on top of mean temperature (BIO1) and total annual precipitation (BIO12).

plotly::plot_ly(
  data   = env3_df,
  x      = ~bio1, y = ~bio12, z = ~bio15,
  type   = "scatter3d", mode = "markers",
  marker = list(size = 2, opacity = 0.5)
) |>
  plotly::layout(scene = list(
    xaxis = list(title = "BIO1 (°C)"),
    yaxis = list(title = "BIO12 (mm)"),
    zaxis = list(title = "BIO15 (CV)")
  ))

Step 2 — Define a virtual species’ niche

A nicheR ellipsoid is built from a range data frame that lists the minimum and maximum each environmental variable can take inside the species’ niche. The package converts this into a centroid, a covariance matrix, semi-axes, and a niche volume.

Imagine a hypothetical tropical mammal that tolerates mean annual temperatures between 25 and 28 °C and annual precipitation between 1200 and 2500 mm.

range_df <- data.frame(
  bio1  = c(25, 28),
  bio12 = c(1200, 2500),
  bio15 = c(70, 90)
)

ell <- build_ellipsoid(range = range_df)
ell
## nicheR Ellipsoid Object
## -----------------------
## Dimensions:        3D
## Chi-square cutoff: 11.345
## Centroid (mu):     26.5, 1850, 80
## 
## Covariance matrix:
##       bio1    bio12  bio15
## bio1  0.25     0.00  0.000
## bio12 0.00 46944.44  0.000
## bio15 0.00     0.00 11.111
## 
## Covariance Limits:
##                  min    max
## bio1-bio12   -54.167 107.25
## bio1-bio15    -0.833   1.65
## bio12-bio15 -361.111 715.00
## 
## Ellipsoid semi-axis lengths:
##   729.78, 11.227, 1.684
## 
## Ellipsoid axis endpoints:
##  Axis 1:
##          bio1   bio12 bio15
## vertex_a 26.5 1120.22    80
## vertex_b 26.5 2579.78    80
## 
##  Axis 2:
##          bio1 bio12  bio15
## vertex_a 26.5  1850 68.773
## vertex_b 26.5  1850 91.227
## 
##  Axis 3:
##            bio1 bio12 bio15
## vertex_a 24.816  1850    80
## vertex_b 28.184  1850    80
## 
## Ellipsoid volume:  57800.1

ell carries the centroid, covariance matrix, and dimensions of the niche. We can visualise it directly in E-space, with the available environmental conditions of Thailand as background.

# `env3_df` from Step 1 carries every pixel's (bio1, bio12, bio15) values —
# we use it as the background point cloud here too.
plot_ellipsoid(ell, background = env3_df)

Step 3 — Predict suitability and Mahalanobis distance in G-space

predict() projects the ellipsoid onto a SpatRaster and returns both a suitability surface (1 at the centroid, 0 at the boundary, with truncated = TRUE clamping outside-niche pixels to zero) and a Mahalanobis distance surface.

pred <- predict(ell,
                newdata = env3,
                include_suitability = TRUE,
                suitability_truncated = TRUE,
                include_mahalanobis = TRUE,
                mahalanobis_truncated = TRUE,
                keep_data = TRUE)

# pred is a multi-layer SpatRaster carrying "suitability" and "mahalanobis"
pred
## class       : SpatRaster 
## size        : 89, 50, 7  (nrow, ncol, nlyr)
## resolution  : 0.1666667, 0.1666667  (x, y)
## extent      : 97.33333, 105.6667, 5.666667, 20.5  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
## coord. ref. : lon/lat WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) 
## sources     : bioclim_thailand.tif  (3 layers) 
##               memory  
##               memory  
##               ... and 2 more sources
## names       :     bio1, bio12,     bio15, Mahalanobis,  suitability, Mahal~trunc, ... 
## min values  : 20.52728,   806,  37.47298,   0.8032656, 5.926184e-43,   0.8032656, ... 
## max values  : 28.89861,  4101, 109.94184, 194.4635571, 6.692265e-01,  11.2917557, ...
plot(pred,
     col  = viridis::viridis(100))

# Binary potential distribution map
binary_rast <- (pred$suitability_trunc > 0) * 1

plot(binary_rast,
     axes = FALSE, box = TRUE,
     col  = c("#d4d4d4", "#0004d5"),
     main = "Potential Distribution (Binary)")

Step 4 — Add a sampling-bias layer

Real-world surveys are biased toward accessible places. We can simulate that bias and apply it on top of suitability.

For this lesson we use species-richness and nighttime-lights rasters (written by Day2a_Data_Download.Rmd) as proxies for survey effort.

sp_rich_layer   <- rast("data/processed/bias/sp_richness_TH.tif")
nighttime_layer <- rast("data/processed/bias/nighttime_TH.tif")
bias_layer <- c(sp_rich_layer, nighttime_layer)
plot(bias_layer)

# Combine bias layers into a 0-1 composite surface
bias_prep <- prepare_bias(bias_surface = bias_layer,
                          effect_direction = c("direct", "inverse"))

plot(bias_prep$composite_surface)

biased_pred <- apply_bias(prepared_bias = bias_prep,
                          prediction = pred,
                          prediction_layer = "suitability",
                          effect_direction = "direct")

plot(biased_pred$suitability_biased,
     main = "Suitability × bias  =  realised sampling surface")

plot(pred$suitability)

Step 5 — Generate virtual occurrence points with three sampling strategies

nicheR::sample_data() accepts three sampling strategies:

  • "centroid" — points weighted toward the niche centroid (preferred conditions).
  • "random" — points drawn uniformly from inside the niche.
  • "edge" — points biased toward the niche periphery (marginal conditions).

We draw 50 virtual records under each strategy and compare them.

pts_centroid <- sample_data(n_occ = 50,
                            prediction = pred,
                            sampling = "centroid",
                            prediction_layer = "suitability",
                            seed = 123)

pts_random   <- sample_data(n_occ = 50,
                            prediction = pred,
                            sampling = "random",
                            prediction_layer = "suitability",
                            seed = 123)

pts_edge     <- sample_data(n_occ = 50,
                            prediction = pred,
                            sampling = "edge",
                            prediction_layer = "suitability",
                            seed = 123)

head(pts_centroid)
##              x         y     bio1 bio12    bio15 Mahalanobis suitability
## 2229 102.08333 13.083333 25.89022  1813 77.81184   1.9474173 0.377679747
## 3767 100.08333  7.916667 27.37949  1975 74.68124   5.9728751 0.050466901
## 2277 101.75000 12.916667 26.94364  1797 76.10129   2.2150842 0.330369987
## 1926 101.58333 14.083333 27.58408  1437 80.01056   8.3343812 0.015495733
## 820  100.58333 17.750000 26.19143  1357 87.12326  10.1249194 0.006329971
## 1860  98.91667 14.250000 26.46350  1848 82.97742   0.8032656 0.669226456
##      Mahalanobis_trunc suitability_trunc
## 2229         1.9474173       0.377679747
## 3767         5.9728751       0.050466901
## 2277         2.2150842       0.330369987
## 1926         8.3343812       0.015495733
## 820         10.1249194       0.006329971
## 1860         0.8032656       0.669226456
head(pts_random)
##              x         y     bio1 bio12    bio15 Mahalanobis  suitability
## 1022 100.91667 17.083333 26.22702  1432 90.70489    14.33352 7.718200e-04
## 2325 101.41667 12.750000 27.23234  1550 71.73042    10.21721 6.044505e-03
## 1309  98.75000 16.083333 23.94322  1795 91.21496    37.53275 7.077303e-09
## 3507  98.41667  8.750000 26.85569  3179 69.95949    47.20319 5.622852e-11
## 3913  99.41667  7.416667 27.18116  2029 53.59823    65.27329 6.700176e-15
## 316   99.91667 19.416667 25.05855  1202 84.03809    18.72335 8.595599e-05
##      Mahalanobis_trunc suitability_trunc
## 1022                NA       0.000000000
## 2325          10.21721       0.006044505
## 1309                NA       0.000000000
## 3507                NA       0.000000000
## 3913                NA       0.000000000
## 316                 NA       0.000000000
head(pts_edge)
##             x        y     bio1 bio12    bio15 Mahalanobis  suitability
## 120  100.5833 20.08333 24.26546  1591 92.29397   35.004386 2.505498e-08
## 1434 102.9167 15.75000 27.15978  1113 81.04131   13.409294 1.225205e-03
## 731  102.4167 18.08333 25.37893  1605 95.23872   27.205518 1.237077e-06
## 1324 101.2500 16.08333 26.28291  1145 81.49200   10.976376 4.135330e-03
## 1928 101.9167 14.08333 27.33204  1336 78.42590    8.620012 1.343347e-02
## 4427 101.7500  5.75000 25.32706  2460 45.87735  118.221515 2.130709e-26
##      Mahalanobis_trunc suitability_trunc
## 120                 NA        0.00000000
## 1434                NA        0.00000000
## 731                 NA        0.00000000
## 1324         10.976376        0.00413533
## 1928          8.620012        0.01343347
## 4427                NA        0.00000000

A bias-weighted draw uses sample_biased_data() together with the realised sampling surface.

biased_pts <- sample_biased_data(
  n_occ            = 50,
  prediction       = biased_pred,
  prediction_layer = "suitability_biased_direct",
  seed             = 123
)

head(biased_pts)
##              x        y suitability_biased_direct
## 2179 102.08333 13.25000               0.169876848
## 4279 102.08333  6.25000               0.032600839
## 2277 101.75000 12.91667               0.147275526
## 2077 101.75000 13.58333               0.009762376
## 1690 103.91667 14.91667               0.003202499
## 1860  98.91667 14.25000               0.434026880
occ_bias_env <- terra::extract(env3, biased_pts[, c("x", "y")])
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))

plot(pred[["suitability"]],
     main = "Centroid sample (n = 50)")
points(pts_centroid[, c("x", "y")], pch = 19, cex = 0.6, col = "red")

plot(pred[["suitability"]],
     main = "Random sample (n = 50)")
points(pts_random[, c("x", "y")], pch = 19, cex = 0.6, col = "red")

plot(pred[["suitability"]],
     main = "Edge sample (n = 50)")
points(pts_edge[, c("x", "y")], pch = 19, cex = 0.6, col = "red")

plot(biased_pred$suitability_biased,
     main = "Bias-weighted sample (n = 50)")
points(biased_pts[, c("x", "y")], pch = 19, cex = 0.6, col = "red")

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

The three strategies populate the niche differently in E-space:

plot_ellipsoid(ell, background = env3_df)
points(pts_centroid[, c("bio1", "bio12")], col = "blue",      pch = 19, cex = 0.6)
points(pts_random[,   c("bio1", "bio12")], col = "darkgreen", pch = 17, cex = 0.6)
points(pts_edge[,     c("bio1", "bio12")], col = "purple",    pch = 15, cex = 0.6)
points(occ_bias_env[, c("bio1", "bio12")], col = "red",       pch = 4,  cex = 0.6)
legend("topright",
       legend = c("Centroid", "Random", "Edge", "Bias-weighted"),
       col    = c("blue", "darkgreen", "purple", "red"),
       pch    = c(19, 17, 15, 4),
       bty    = "n")

Wrap-up

What we just did:

  • Defined a niche a priori as a 2-D ellipsoid in bioclim space.
  • Explored the available environmental space of Thailand in 3D using BIO1, BIO12, BIO15.
  • Projected the ellipsoid to Thailand and inspected suitability + Mahalanobis distance.
  • Added a sampling-bias surface and contrasted three sampling strategies (centroid, random, edge) plus a bias-weighted draw.

On this page

Developed by Paanwaris Paansri, Luis E. Escobar.

Site built with pkgdown 2.1.3.
